segments.text: Welcome to the chapter on evaluating foundation models trained with Earth observation data.
This video is presented by Lillianne Thomas on behalf of her co-authors Wei-Zhi Liang, Daniel Weismann, and Soumya Ranjan.
In this video accompaniment, we'll go over the code and concepts that demonstrate how you can explore the ways foundation models are transforming Earth observation,
from rapid flood mapping during disasters to monitoring deforestation and assessing agricultural health.
By the end of this chapter, we hope you'll understand what foundation models are,
how they work, and most importantly, how to use them for practical earth science applications.
In this video, we will first cover the theory and concepts in the slides that follow,
and then we'll proceed into the interactive section where we'll actually walk through the
notebook that accompanies these slides for this chapter. So stay tuned after these slides to get
a walkthrough of the code in the notebook.
Here's what we'll cover today.
First, we'll define what Earth Observation Foundation models actually are
and how they learn from satellite imagery.
Then we'll get hands-on with two different models,
PRYTHV from NASA and IBM, and CLAY from the Clay Foundation.
We'll apply these models to a real disaster event, the 2022 Pakistan floods,
and see how their embeddings capture meaningful patterns.
Finally, we'll train simple classifiers on top of these embeddings to detect flood conditions.
So what exactly is a foundation model for Earth observation?
It's a large-scale machine learning model that's been pre-trained on vast amounts of
remote sensing data, including optical imagery, radar, thermal, and multispectral, to learn
general-purpose representations of the Earth's surface.
The key insight is that these models learn without human-annotated labels.
They use a technique called self-supervised learning, where the model creates its own training signal from the data itself.
A common approach is masked reconstruction, where you hide parts of an image and train the model to predict what's missing.
This forces the model to learn the underlying structure and patterns in the data.
Most foundation models use an architecture called a vision transformer, or VIT for short.
We'll explain more on how that works in the next slide.
But generally, the most popular methods for developing EOFMs, or Earth Observation Foundation models, is as follows.
First, masked reconstruction. This is what Perthvy and Clay use.
You randomly hide or mask parts of an image, then train the model to predict the missing content.
Think of it like a jigsaw puzzle. To fill in the gaps, the model must understand what agricultural fields look like, how water bodies appear, and how urban areas are structured.
It learns the underlying patterns of Earth's surface.
The second method that I'll mention is contrastive learning.
Here, the model learns to distinguish between similar and dissimilar pairs.

You might take two augmented views of the same image, rotated, cropped, color shifted, and train the model to recognize they're actually the same scene while distinguishing them from completely different locations.
This teaches the model what makes a scene ultimately unique.
The third method worth mentioning today is generative methods.
These are used to train the model to synthesize or reconstruct entire input distributions.
The model learns to generate realistic satellite imagery, which requires understanding the
full complexity of what Earth's surface looks like.
The key insight across all of these approaches is that no labeled data is required for pre-training.
The supervision signal comes entirely from the data itself.
This is why foundation models can leverage the petabytes of unlabeled satellite imagery
that already exist.
For a VIT, the idea is simple but powerful.
You divide an image into fixed-size patches, usually 16 by 16 pixels, and treat each patch
as a token, similar to words in a sentence.
Then apply the transformer's self-attention mechanism to model relationships between patches.
This allows the model to capture both local details and global context.
It's worth noting that vision transformers are the backbone architecture.
They're not the foundation models themselves, but rather the building blocks used to construct
them.
Why should you care about foundation models?
First, reduce training costs.
Traditional models require training from scratch for every application.
Foundation models are pre-trained, so you only need to fine-tune them with smaller datasets.
Secondly, faster development.
You get to use ready-to-use features and embeddings, eliminating extensive pre-processing.
Third, improved accuracy with limited data.
Because they've learned from massive datasets, foundation models are robust even when you
only have a handful of labeled examples.
Fourth, scalability.
One model can be repurposed for many tasks, including flood mapping, land cover classification,
vegetation health, and more.
Fifth, democratizing access.
Many foundation models are open source, so smaller organizations can access state-of-the-art
tools without expensive infrastructure.
Sixth, better backbones than ImageNet.
These models are trained on actual satellite imagery with multispectral bands, not just
RGB photos.
That means red, green, blue, natural color photos.
And seventh, this is important.
While foundation models may not beat highly specialized bespoke models, they can get you
approximately 90% of the way there with about 10% of the effort.
That in and of itself is a compelling trade-off for many applications.
Of course, foundation models aren't perfect.
They're still relatively new in the Earth observation research community and do need
more validation across diverse applications.
As well, there are domain-specific challenges.
Models may struggle with conditions that weren't well represented in their training data, such
as unusual crop varieties, rapidly changing urban areas, or the unique spectral signatures
of polar regions.
Earth observation data is also inherently complex.
We have diverse sensors capturing different wavelengths at different resolutions, temporal
dynamics, varying coordinate systems, and metadata across providers.
No single model today fully handles all of this complexity.
And here's the good news, we don't need one monolithic model.
For example, you might use one model for temporal analysis of change over time, one for flexible
multi-sensor fusion, and another for more specialized data.
This tactical thinking aligns with how the field is evolving.
Rather than waiting for one perfect model, we can build systems that leverage the strengths
of multiple models.
We'll apply foundation models to one of the most catastrophic flood events in recent history, the 2022 Pakistan floods.
Between approximately July and August 2022, unprecedented monsoon rainfall triggered massive flooding across Pakistan.
Approximately 33 million people were affected, vast agricultural lands were submerged, and the World Bank estimated over 30 billion US dollars in damages.
This is exactly the kind of scenario where rapid, accurate flood mapping is critical, and where foundation models can make a real difference.
Our study area is centered on Patadan and Pakistan's Sindh province, one of the regions most severely impacted by the floods.
During this period, the Indus River and its tributaries overflowed, submerging the surrounding agricultural lands.
We'll analyze satellite imagery from June through October 2022, capturing pre-flood conditions, active flooding, and the post-flood recovery.
We will work with two different foundation models.
Prithvi, developed by NASA and IBM, is a temporal vision transformer trained on harmonized Landsat
Sentinel-2 data.
It's optimized for spatiotemporal analysis and processes sequences of four images, what
we call quadruplets.
CLAE from the CLAE Foundation is designed for flexibility.
It supports variable image resolutions and sensor types and processes individual images
rather than temporal sequences.
By applying both models, we'll see how different architectural choices can effectively capture
flood signatures.
The Prithvi HLS Foundation model was innovatively developed using a masked autoencoder approach
combined with temporal modeling.
The model uses six spectral bands,
blue, green, red, near infrared,
shortwave infrared one, and shortwave infrared two.
It processes sequences of four images,
which allows it to capture temporal dynamics
like seasonal changes or in our case, flood progression.
Notice the architecture diagram shows 3D patch embeddings.
This exemplifies how Prithvi encodes both spatial and temporal features.
Input data is formatted with the following dimensions, bands, channels, time, height, and width.
This five-dimensional structure is key to understanding how Prithvi works.
Once formatted as a temporal sequence, the quadruplet of images are converted to 3D patch embeddings,
processed by the transformer encoder, after which we extract the class token, CLS, as our final embedding.
Here's our data pipeline.
First, we define location and dates.
We're using Paddan, Pakistan from June through October 2022.
We query the stack catalog.
In this case, we engage NASA's CMR endpoint and request the HLS-30 collection.
We'll download imagery. We get 22 images for this time period, each with six bands at 256 by 256 pixels in height and width.
We will format the imagery for the model. This is where we will create, in the case of Prithvi, quadruplets with overlapping sequences of four images.
And then we will generate embeddings. The Prithvi model outputs 1024 dimensional vectors, one per quadruplet.
From there, we'll analyze embeddings with principal component analysis and TES&E, as well as train a classifier on top of the embeddings.
The imagery we will download breaks down generally into three phases of interest.
Pre-flood encompassing June 2022, for which we have five images showing normal conditions.
In these, you can see agricultural fields and dry land.
The next phase is July through August 2022.
That is what we are considering the active flooding period.
And for that, we have four images where you'll notice some dark pixels.
These are likely to represent water inundation covering the landscape.
The last phase is late August through October 2022, which we consider the post-flood recovery phase.
and this entails 13 images showing gradual recovery, though some standing water may persist.
A visual inspection such as this is important.
It helps us understand what patterns we expect the model's embeddings to capture.
The question that we want to ask is, can the model learn to distinguish these phases without us telling the model which is which?
As mentioned, we'll run a principal components analysis on our embeddings.
Why do we want to do something such as this?
Well, our embeddings are 1024-dimensional vectors, which is very difficult to visualize directly.
PCA extracts the directions of maximum variance in the data, allowing us to project down to one or two dimensions for visualization.
What we can expect to see? Similar conditions should cluster together in PCA space. Different conditions should also separate.
separate. If the model has learned meaningful representations, pre-flood images should cluster
together, flood images should form a separate group, and post-flood should be distinct.
A cloudy image might deviate from the groups as well. Let's see if that's what happens.
Our first approach for PCA and Prithvi embeddings uses the class token for a given quadruplet.
We'll pass each quadruplet through Prithvi, extract the class token which summarizes all
four time steps, and with those we'll get 19 embeddings, one per quadruplet. The result
visualized in this plot shows some separation between flood phases, which is encouraging,
but there's an issue with how we're using these embeddings. It's hard to cluster temporally when
a given time step can exist in multiple quadruplets. Image 5, for example, appears in
quadruplets 2, 3, 4, and 5. This creates temporal leakage that can compromise our analysis.
we need to do some further processing. So instead of using the class tokens, we'll show how to do
PCA using individual timesteps. Prithvi outputs two things. The first is per quadruplet the class
token, and that's ultimately a summary of the entire quadruplet of timesteps. So it's a summary
embedding if you will. Prithvi also outputs patch tokens for each time step. That would be something
more along the lines of a per time step embedding and so this is ultimately what we want to use to
get a nice clustering of the different flood phases. To get patch tokens for each time step,
we'll start with embeddings that are of shape one which is the batch size.
we're processing one quadruplet at a time. NUM tokens and that means the number of tokens which
relates to total number of patch tokens output by the transformer. This includes patches from
all four time steps combined. Embedding dimension which for Prithvi is 1024.
We reshape that to T which is our number of time steps in the quadruplet so four.
patches per time step which defines how many spatial patches come from each image for a 256
by 256 image with 16 by 16 patches that's 256 patches per time step lastly a new shape is the
embedding dimension which remains 1024 we then average across patches to get t which again is 4
by embedding dimension, which is 1024.
That ultimately is a shape that is a representation
of how we collapse 256 spatial patches into a single vector per time step.
And that final shape is ultimately a representation
of four embeddings of 1024 dimensions each.
so that is what you can consider one embedding per image in the quadruplet.
The result here is we have now processed the embeddings
so that we have one embedding per time step with no mixing between images.
Each embedding represents exactly one point in time.
So, as we can see, PCA on the per-time step embeddings gives us a fairly clear separation
of flood phases.
If we plot PCA component 1 against time, we can see how distinct clustering emerges.
We see pre-flood images grouped together, flood images separate out, and that includes
the cloudy image.
and we see post-flood images form their own cluster.
The key insight here is that the model distinguishes flood phases without any labels.
To generate this result, we never instruct the model on which images are pre-flood, during
flood, or post-flood.
The self-supervised pre-training produces representations that naturally separate these
conditions.
Both shows how foundation models accelerate analysis through the meaningful patterns they've
already learned from their massive pre-training datasets.
We'll proceed to look at another dimensionality reduction technique called T-SNE.
The difference between PCA and T-SNE is that PCA is a linear projection that preserves
global variance, and T-SNE is nonlinear and preserves local neighborhoods, so similar
things stay close together. Our TS&E results confirm the PCA findings. We see fairly distinct
clusters for each flood phase. The fact that both methods show similar patterns gives us confidence
that what we're seeing is genuine structure in the embeddings, not artifacts of a particular
visualization technique. Go from embeddings to predictions by training a very lightweight
classifier. The process will be super simple. We'll use our per-timestep embeddings as features,
upon which we'll assign labels to each timestep. 0 for cloudy, 1 for pre-flood, 2 for flood,
3 for post-flood. We'll partition timesteps into training and testing sets,
with an effort to include samples from each phase in both partitions.
We'll train a simple support vector machine classifier as our last step.
As you'll see in the results in the notebook, 9 out of 10 embeddings from the test set are
correctly predicted. That shows pretty strong classification and performance with such minimal
data. In reflection, although we may only have 22 samples, the support vector machine trained
minimally on these embeddings proves to accurately classify flood phases. This really demonstrates
the transfer learning power of foundation models. The hard work of learning visual representations
was already done during pre-training. In an example such as this, we really just had to add
a thin classifier on top. Now let's look at CLE and understand the key difference from Prithvi.
CLE processes individual images, not quadruplets. One image goes in, one embedding comes out.
There's no temporal mixing. This means for clay, embeddings are directly per time step. We don't
need to do the extraction we did with Prithvi. We get 22 embeddings directly, one for each of our 22 images.
PCA on clay embeddings also shows clear flood separation, flood phase separation. The clustering
pattern is slightly different from Prithvi, which indicates how it emphasizes different aspects of
the data based on its different training. The key insight however is that both models capture flood
dynamics but with different learned representations. The practical implication we want to consider is
to use an ensemble of multiple models for more robust predictions. If both Prithvi
and Clay agree that an image shows flooding you can be pretty confident in that classification.
This table captures the key differences between the two models. As for input format, Prithvi
requires quadruplets, those sequences of four consecutive images. Clay takes single images.
This has practical implications. For example, if you only have one cloud-free image from a flood
event, Clay can work with it directly, while Prithvi needs a temporal sequence.
As for temporal context, Prithvi uses temporal modeling across those four timesteps.
Clay doesn't encode temporal context this way.
Each image is processed independently.
Embedding extraction.
With Prithvi, we needed that extra step to extract per-time step embeddings from the
patch tokens.
With clay, we get one embedding per image directly.
Clustering quality.
Both achieved excellent separation of flood phases in our analysis.
Neither model is clearly better for this task.
So when should you use each?
PrisB might be your choice when temporal dynamics are foremost in importance.
Monitoring gradual changes, detecting the onset of recession of events, or when you
have good temporal coverage.
They could be better for flexible applications, such as working with different sensors, variable
resolutions, or when you only have single images available.
In practice, you might use both and compare results, or even ensemble them for more robust
predictions.
Let's recap what we've learned in this chapter.
Foundation models learn general representations without labels.
No manual annotation is required for pre-training.
Self-supervised learning enables training on vast unlabeled data, leveraging the petabytes
of satellite imagery that already exist.
Embeddings capture meaningful patterns.
Flood phases cluster naturally without any supervision.
Different models have different strengths.
Prithvi is great for temporal.
Clay is awesome for flexibility.
Simple classifiers on top of embeddings achieve strong results.
A support vector machine with only 22 samples works impressively well.
Practical applications are accessible with just a few lines of code.
You can adapt this workflow to your own use cases.
You'll benefit from keeping in mind some limitations, such as
Models may struggle with underrepresented conditions.
If something wasn't in the training data, the model may not handle it too well.
Earth observation foundation models need validation across diverse geographies.
What works in Pakistan may need verification in other regions.
Computational requirements for inference.
These are large models that require significant computational resources.
Future directions for OFO model development include
Improved multi-sensor fusion models Improved temporal modeling
Smaller, more efficient architectures for edge deployment
More domain-specific fine-tuning for particular applications
The reference at the bottom of this slide provides a comprehensive review of Earth Foundation
